questions.question: Explain the basic principle of Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) and list two practical applications where FDM is still used.
keyPoints: FDM divides the total bandwidth into distinct, non-overlapping frequency sub-bands
Each source is allocated a unique frequency band and can transmit simultaneously
Applications: analog telephone carrier systems, FM radio broadcasting
Requires bandpass filters at the receiver to separate the channels

questions.question: What is Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)? Distinguish between synchronous TDM and statistical TDM in terms of slot allocation and efficiency.
keyPoints: TDM interleaves samples from multiple sources into sequential time slots
Synchronous TDM assigns fixed slots to each source regardless of data availability, leading to potential idle slots
Statistical (asynchronous) TDM dynamically allocates slots based on actual data demand, improving bandwidth utilization
Synchronous TDM requires precise clock synchronization; statistical TDM includes address bits to identify the source

questions.question: Compare and contrast Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) and Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) in terms of principle, hardware requirements, susceptibility to interference, and typical usage scenarios in modern communication networks.
keyPoints: FDM splits the available bandwidth into separate frequency bands; TDM splits time into discrete slots
FDM needs bandpass filters, oscillators, and careful frequency planning; TDM requires precise timing circuits and synchronization
FDM suffers from intermodulation distortion and crosstalk; TDM is prone to timing jitter and clock drift
FDM is used in analog TV, cable TV, and some microwave links; TDM underlies digital telephone hierarchies (PCM) and SONET/SDH

questions.question: Describe Wave Division Multiplexing (WDM) and its two main variants (CWDM and DWDM). Explain how WDM increases the capacity of optical fibers and discuss the advantages and challenges of deploying DWDM in long-haul telecommunication networks.
keyPoints: WDM combines multiple optical signals on a single fiber using different wavelengths (colors) of light
Coarse WDM (CWDM) uses fewer, widely spaced wavelengths (up to 18 channels); Dense WDM (DWDM) packs many tightly spaced channels (up to 80+)
WDM multiplies the fiber capacity linearly—each wavelength can carry rates from 10 Gbps to 100 Gbps or more
Advantages: huge capacity, low attenuation, protocol transparency; challenges: need for precise lasers, dispersion management, cost of optical amplifiers and multiplexers
